rationale_for_baseline_tier: A benign, self-limiting large local reaction to an insect bite with all dangerous mimics screened negative (no systemic allergic features, no spreading infection, systemically well). This is comfortably community/telehealth-manageable with symptomatic treatment and safety-netting — T1 (routine community management, arrangeable now via telehealth with a short topical-steroid script and pharmacy antihistamine). The source note's urgent in-person home visit reflects telephone-triage uncertainty and the patient's high anxiety, not true clinical acuity. There is no indication for ED, an ambulance, or same-day urgent assessment for the current presentation; escalation applies only if the safety-net triggers below appear.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any difficulty breathing, swelling of the face/lips/tongue, a sensation of the throat closing, a widespread rash/hives, or sudden severe dizziness or faintness
escalation_edges.rationale: These would signal a systemic allergic reaction / anaphylaxis, which is immediately life-threatening and needs adrenaline and an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 straight away if you get any trouble breathing, swelling of your face, lips or tongue, a feeling that your throat is closing, a rash spreading over your body, or sudden dizziness or faintness.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A red line or streak spreading up the leg, the area becoming hot and increasingly painful rather than itchy, or feeling generally unwell with fever or chills
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day medical review (GP, urgent care or after-hours service)
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate an evolving bacterial skin infection (cellulitis) that needs prompt assessment and antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: See a doctor the same day if you notice a red line spreading up your leg, the lump becomes hot and more painful than itchy, or you feel unwell with a fever or chills.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The lesion rapidly blisters, blackens or breaks down, or the pain becomes severe and out of proportion
escalation_edges.rationale: Blistering, necrosis or disproportionate pain could indicate a medically significant venomous bite or tissue damage needing urgent in-person evaluation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bite quickly blisters, turns dark or breaks down, or the pain becomes severe, get seen in person urgently the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The lump is not settling after about 3 days of treatment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Book a routine GP review within a few days
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure to improve on symptomatic treatment warrants a routine GP reassessment to reconsider the diagnosis and management.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it hasn't started settling after about 3 days of the antihistamine and ointment, book in to see your GP.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone; highly anxious about a venomous bite / allergic reaction
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Because she is alone, give explicit, memorable safety-net triggers and encourage her to keep her phone to hand and, if she can, ask someone to check in over the next few hours. Acknowledge and address the anxiety with clear reassurance rather than reinforcing catastrophic fears — but make the anaphylaxis and cellulitis triggers unmistakable so a genuine deterioration is acted on promptly.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a benign, localised reaction to an insect bite and can be managed at home. Take a non-sedating antihistamine (such as cetirizine) for the itch, use a cold compress when the itch is bad, keep the area clean and try hard not to scratch it. A short course of a steroid ointment will help it settle faster. It should ease over 24-48 hours and clear over a few days.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Regular GP within about 3 days if not settling; use an after-hours GP or urgent care for same-day concerns; call 000 for any breathing or whole-body allergic symptoms.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If a same-day concern arises after hours (spreading redness, fever, increasing pain), use an after-hours GP service or urgent care; call 000 immediately for any breathing difficulty or face/lip/tongue/throat swelling.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Localised large local reaction to an arthropod bite on the right lateral calf; systemic allergic and infective features screened negative; managed with oral antihistamine and a short course of topical corticosteroid, with safety-netting for anaphylaxis and cellulitis. GP to review in ~3 days if not settling.
